hyperedges.label: Offline-First Sync Pipeline (client outbox to hypertable)
Orchestrator Delegation Workflow (brief, implement, review, document)
Backend Local Compose Stack (db → migrate → api startup chain)

nodes.label: docs-site GitHub Workflow (MkDocs build + Pages deploy)
nodes.norm_label: docs-site github workflow (mkdocs build + pages deploy)

nodes.label: Orchestrator Role
nodes.rationale: The main session defines work, delegates, and triages; it never writes code or docs itself. Atomic tasks (one Linear issue, one PR, one agent, no mid-task decisions) keep briefs complete and reviews severity-triaged (HIGH fix immediately, MEDIUM flag human, LOW batch into cleanup ticket).

nodes.label: documentation/ Is the Source of Truth
nodes.rationale: Code must conform to documentation contracts (database-schema, api-reference, sync-protocol); contract changes update the doc in the same cycle so every client and the backend are checked against a single shared specification rather than another codebase's implementation.
nodes.norm_label: documentation/ is the source of truth

nodes.label: Git Flow: One Ticket, One Branch, One PR
nodes.rationale: Never work on main; always branch feat/RIZ-<n>-<slug> from fresh origin/main; Conventional Commits referencing RIZ-<n>; PR titled [RIZ-<n>] merges into submodule main followed by a pointer-bump commit in the master repo. Keeps work atomic and traceable to Linear.
nodes.norm_label: git flow: one ticket, one branch, one pr

nodes.label: Containerization Requirement
nodes.rationale: Every containerizable project provides a Dockerfile and runs end-to-end with docker compose; rize-desktop and rize-mobile are exempt because macOS/iOS GUI apps cannot run in Linux containers.

nodes.label: Offline-First Clients
nodes.rationale: Clients record activity and manage sessions locally first, independent of connectivity, because tracking must not be interrupted or lost by transient connectivity loss while capture runs continuously in the background.

nodes.label: Server-Authoritative Aggregation
nodes.rationale: The backend is the source of truth for cross-device aggregation and reporting; unified cross-device reports need a single convergence point that only the backend can provide, so clients never merge each other's data locally.

nodes.label: Client-Generated UUIDv7 Idempotency
nodes.rationale: Offline-first clients may retry uploads after connectivity gaps; UUIDv7 generated on the client before the first network attempt is itself the idempotency key, time-ordered so it stays sortable while letting the backend deduplicate replayed uploads without a round trip to allocate an ID.

nodes.label: Last-Write-Wins Conflict Resolution
nodes.rationale: Mutable entities (focus sessions, projects, tags, settings) reconcile by comparing client-supplied updated_at; LWW is a simple, low-coordination strategy where losing a rare concurrent edit is an acceptable tradeoff against sync complexity.

nodes.label: Append-Only Immutable Activity Events
nodes.rationale: Raw activity events are never mutated after ingestion; corrections append new events. Matches the append-heavy time-series nature of the data and keeps the ingestion path simple and auditable.

nodes.label: activity_events TimescaleDB Hypertable
nodes.rationale: Highest-volume table, partitioned on started_at with 7-day chunks balancing write-heavy recent access against chunk-granular compression (30 days) and retention. The PK is composite (user_id, started_at, event_id) because TimescaleDB can only enforce uniqueness when the constraint includes the partitioning column; the UNIQUE (user_id, event_id, started_at) constraint is the idempotency guarantee for retried uploads.

nodes.label: TimescaleDB Continuous Aggregates (daily_app_totals, daily_category_totals, hourly_category_totals)
nodes.rationale: Chosen over hand-rolled rollup jobs for three reasons: automatic refresh without a backend scheduler; correctness under late/out-of-order inserts from offline-first clients (incremental re-materialization of affected buckets); and transparent querying over compressed and uncompressed chunks. Real-time aggregation unions materialized buckets with live rows so 'today' is always correct.

nodes.label: server_seq Keyset Pagination
nodes.rationale: Syncable tables bump a server_seq BIGINT on every write; pulls page with WHERE server_seq > :cursor ORDER BY server_seq, which stays correct and efficient regardless of accumulated data, unlike offset pagination.

nodes.label: refresh_tokens Table (Rotation Families)
nodes.rationale: Each rotation produces a new token in the same family_id; presenting a revoked or already-rotated token revokes the entire family — the standard defense against a stolen refresh token replayed after the legitimate client has rotated past it. token_hash index makes per-request validation a single indexed lookup.

nodes.label: deletion_requests Table (GDPR Grace Period)
nodes.rationale: Implements the GDPR-mandated grace period between a deletion request and execution so a request can be cancelled within the window; executed_at IS NULL distinguishes pending, cancellable requests.

nodes.label: Sync Push (POST /v1/sync/events)
nodes.rationale: Clients push their local outbox in batches of at most 500 items; per-item results (applied/duplicate/invalid) allow partial success so one invalid item never rejects a whole batch.

nodes.label: Sync Pull (GET /v1/sync/changes)
nodes.rationale: Keyset pagination over server_seq with an opaque cursor makes pulls idempotent and safe to repeat: the same cursor returns the same page and re-applying it is a no-op, which is what makes cursor loss and full re-pull after device restore safe without special-cased recovery logic. Applying pulled changes and persisting the new cursor must share one local transaction.

nodes.label: Clock Skew Handling (24h Bound)
nodes.rationale: If a client's clock is skewed more than 24 hours from server time, the server substitutes its own time before the LWW comparison, bounding how far a misconfigured client clock can distort ordering across devices at the cost of that write being ordered by arrival time.

nodes.label: Precision Semantics (exact vs approximate)
nodes.rationale: exact events reflect directly observed boundaries (desktop continuous observation); approximate events derive from iOS DeviceActivityMonitor threshold callbacks that only signal usage crossing a boundary, not precise start/stop instants. Reports can filter by precision to scope to strictly-observed time.

nodes.label: Modular Monolith Service Shape
nodes.rationale: One Go binary, one database, package-enforced module boundaries (handlers -> services -> repositories -> PostgreSQL). Chosen over microservices for transactional consistency across ingestion/resolution/aggregation, low operational cost for a small team, and boundaries that preserve the option to extract services later.

nodes.label: Backend Middleware Stack (Request ID, Logging, Recoverer, CORS, Rate limit, Auth, RBAC)
nodes.rationale: Ordering is deliberate: request ID and logging wrap everything so every request is traceable; the recoverer sits outside business logic so any downstream panic is caught; CORS and rate limiting reject abusive traffic before any per-user work; RBAC only makes sense after auth establishes the caller.
nodes.norm_label: backend middleware stack (request id, logging, recoverer, cors, rate limit, auth, rbac)

nodes.label: Ingestion Pipeline (validate, app catalog resolution, category resolution, idempotent upsert)
nodes.rationale: Batch validation happens before any database work so malformed batches fail cheaply; the app catalog grows organically as unseen bundle_ids arrive; categories resolve via user override then app default; the final upsert dedupes retries by client UUIDv7, never edits committed events.
nodes.norm_label: ingestion pipeline (validate, app catalog resolution, category resolution, idempotent upsert)

nodes.label: Forward-Only Migration Policy (golang-migrate)
nodes.rationale: Every schema change ships as a new numbered migration, never edited or reverted in place, keeping history a linear replayable audit trail — important because hypertables and continuous aggregates carry TimescaleDB-specific steps not straightforward to express as automatic down migrations.

nodes.label: Desktop Tracking Engine
nodes.rationale: Combines event-driven NSWorkspace didActivateApplicationNotification for app switches with a 1-5s polling loop for window titles (Accessibility API primary, CGWindowList fallback needing Screen Recording); a 5-second minimum-duration filter discards focus-flicker noise before writing immutable events.

nodes.label: Desktop Tracking State Machine (active/idle/locked/sleeping)
nodes.rationale: active->idle when CGEventSource secondsSinceLastEventType exceeds a 300s default; locked via com.apple.screenIsLocked; sleeping via NSWorkspace willSleep/didWake. Returning to active triggers a back-fill decision: discard the gap or prompt the user to attribute it.

nodes.label: Desktop Event Store (SQLite via GRDB, outbox)
nodes.rationale: Offline-first local database holding immutable activity_events plus a SQLite outbox table; the local schema mirrors the server ingest contract in database-schema.md and must be kept in sync with it.
nodes.norm_label: desktop event store (sqlite via grdb, outbox)

nodes.label: Desktop Sync Client (60s / 500-event flush)
nodes.rationale: Flushes the outbox every 60 seconds or once 500 events accumulate, whichever comes first, with exponential backoff on failure; rows are marked synced only after server acknowledgment — no optimistic completion.

nodes.label: Three-Tier Hybrid Screen Time Model (Tier A/B/C)
nodes.rationale: Exact per-app Screen Time data can only be read inside the network- and file-isolated DeviceActivityReport extension, so exact data can be displayed but never exported or synced. This OS-enforced boundary forces three tiers: A exact display-only, B approximate (±5 min) synced via threshold events, C manual timers/focus sessions exact and synced. UX must label Tier B data approximate and show provenance in blended reports.
nodes.norm_label: three-tier hybrid screen time model (tier a/b/c)

nodes.label: DeviceActivityReport Extension (Tier A)
nodes.rationale: Renders exact per-app usage as a SwiftUI view composited into the host app, but is deliberately network- and file-isolated by iOS: nothing it computes can be written to the App Group, sent over the network, or persisted for sync — an architectural dead end that exists purely for accurate in-the-moment display.

nodes.label: DeviceActivityMonitor Extension (Tier B thresholds)
nodes.rationale: Configured with a daily DeviceActivitySchedule and DeviceActivityEvent thresholds firing at 5-minute increments per tracked app/category; each callback writes a coarse usage record to the App Group. Woken by the system only on threshold crossings, keeping battery cost low; the only synced source of per-app breakdown data, hence the ±5 min approximate precision.

nodes.label: App Group Container group.com.rizeclone.shared
nodes.rationale: The only channel through which Tier B data crosses from the monitor extension into the main app; an on-device shared-container handoff, not a network call.

nodes.label: Token Model (JWT 15 min access + opaque 30-day refresh)
nodes.rationale: Short-lived RS256/EdDSA JWT access tokens limit the exposure window and carry exactly sub, role, device_id so requests can be authorized and scoped without a database round trip; refresh tokens are opaque (not JWTs) precisely so they can be stored hashed at rest — the backend only ever compares hashes. Exactly one active refresh token per device; both tokens stored only in Keychain on clients.
nodes.norm_label: token model (jwt 15 min access + opaque 30-day refresh)

nodes.label: Refresh-Token Rotation with Family Reuse Detection
nodes.rationale: Every refresh rotates the token within the same family; reuse of an already-rotated token is treated as theft and revokes the entire family for that device, so a stolen refresh token is usable at most once before detection bounds the blast radius.
nodes.norm_label: refresh-token rotation with family reuse detection

nodes.label: Sign in with Apple (primary auth)
nodes.rationale: Identity token verified against Apple's JWKS (iss/aud/exp validated); accounts are linked by the token's sub claim stored as apple_user_id, never by email alone, because Apple-provided emails can be relay addresses and are not a stable key. Email/password (argon2id: 64 MiB, 3 iterations, parallelism 4) is secondary; both converge on the same token model.

nodes.label: RBAC (user/admin, user_id scoping)
nodes.rationale: Every query is scoped by user_id from the access token's sub claim at the data-access layer — not left to individual handlers — ruling out cross-tenant access paths; admin routes live under /v1/admin gated by role middleware checking role == admin.

nodes.label: Rate Limiting (token bucket, per-IP auth / per-user sync)
nodes.rationale: Per-IP on /v1/auth/* (suggested 10/min) to blunt credential stuffing; per-user on /v1/sync/* and /v1/reports/* (suggested 60/min) sized for client cadence; brute-force lockout per account is additionally required so attackers cannot evade per-IP limits by distributing across IPs.
nodes.norm_label: rate limiting (token bucket, per-ip auth / per-user sync)

nodes.label: GDPR Data Lifecycle (7-day grace, export, 72h breach)
nodes.rationale: DELETE /v1/users/me creates a deletion_requests row and starts a 7-day grace period before a background job hard-cascade-deletes all user data; POST /v1/users/me/export produces a full JSON dump for portability; breach notification within 72 hours.
nodes.norm_label: gdpr data lifecycle (7-day grace, export, 72h breach)

nodes.label: Certificate Pinning Deliberately Deferred
nodes.rationale: Deferred, not omitted by oversight: pinning adds meaningful operational risk (a mismanaged pin rotation can hard-lock every client out until an app update ships) for marginal gain over TLS 1.2+ and HSTS against the current threat model; revisit if the threat model changes.

nodes.label: Sentry for Error Tracking and Performance
nodes.rationale: Chosen because it satisfies the free requirement twice (SaaS free tier and fully open-source self-hostable) and one platform across all three apps gives a single pane of glass, unified release tagging, and cross-platform issue grouping. GlitchTip retained as an API-compatible fallback (DSN change only). Prometheus/Grafana keep metrics and alerting; Sentry owns errors and traces. Hard rule: never initialize Sentry inside the network-isolated DeviceActivityReport extension. Privacy: sendDefaultPii false, beforeSend scrubs window_title/url/email, pseudonymous UUID identification only, telemetry opt-out toggle.
nodes.norm_label: sentry for error tracking and performance

nodes.label: Backend CI Workflow (lint, test, vuln, docker)
nodes.norm_label: backend ci workflow (lint, test, vuln, docker)

nodes.label: Backend Release Workflow (GoReleaser on v* tags)
nodes.norm_label: backend release workflow (goreleaser on v* tags)

nodes.label: Backend GoReleaser Config (cmd/api, linux/darwin, CGO disabled)
nodes.norm_label: backend goreleaser config (cmd/api, linux/darwin, cgo disabled)

nodes.label: Handlers → Services → Repositories Layering
nodes.rationale: Handlers stay thin (decode, validate, call service, encode) so business logic centralizes in services; data access goes only through sqlc-generated queries — no hand-rolled SQL in services. Errors wrapped with %w context, RFC 7807 bodies at the HTTP boundary.

nodes.label: Per-User Query Scoping (Tenant Isolation)
nodes.rationale: Every query is scoped by user_id from the access token; cross-tenant access paths are a HIGH-severity review finding — the primary data-isolation guarantee of the backend.

nodes.label: TimescaleDB Hypertable + Continuous Aggregates for activity_events
nodes.rationale: activity_events is a hypertable and reports are served from continuous aggregates, so time-bucketed reporting stays cheap at ingestion scale.
nodes.norm_label: timescaledb hypertable + continuous aggregates for activity_events

nodes.label: Backend Docker Compose Stack
nodes.rationale: Containerization is a hard requirement: docker compose up must run db + one-shot migrations + api end-to-end; breaking it is a HIGH-severity review finding.

nodes.label: Desktop CI Workflow (build, test, lint, unsigned .app artifact)
nodes.norm_label: desktop ci workflow (build, test, lint, unsigned .app artifact)

nodes.label: Desktop Release Workflow (unsigned .app on v* tags)
nodes.rationale: Build is intentionally unsigned (CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO); Developer ID signing + notarization is deferred to a later ticket, so the artifact triggers Gatekeeper warnings until that lands.
nodes.norm_label: desktop release workflow (unsigned .app on v* tags)

nodes.label: RizeDesktop XcodeGen Project Spec (macOS 14+, LSUIElement, hardened runtime)
nodes.norm_label: rizedesktop xcodegen project spec (macos 14+, lsuielement, hardened runtime)

nodes.label: Tracking Engine Isolation from UI
nodes.rationale: The tracking engine (NSWorkspace/Accessibility/CGEventSource) must not import AppKit/SwiftUI, keeping native-API tracking components testable and independent of the UI layer (MVVM with @Observable, async/await only).

nodes.label: Offline-First SQLite Outbox with Immutable Events
nodes.rationale: Activity events are immutable once closed: client-generated UUIDv7, sessions under 5s discarded, written to the GRDB/SQLite outbox and marked synced only after server ack — guarantees at-least-once delivery without duplication ambiguity.
nodes.norm_label: offline-first sqlite outbox with immutable events

nodes.label: Developer ID + Notarization Distribution (No App Sandbox)
nodes.rationale: The Accessibility API is incompatible with App Sandbox, so the app skips the Mac App Store and ships via Developer ID + notarization with hardened runtime; when Screen Recording is denied it degrades gracefully to app-name-only tracking.
nodes.norm_label: developer id + notarization distribution (no app sandbox)

nodes.label: Mobile CI Workflow (simulator build, test, lint, unsigned artifact)
nodes.norm_label: mobile ci workflow (simulator build, test, lint, unsigned artifact)

nodes.label: Mobile Release Workflow (unsigned simulator .app, RIZ-25)
nodes.rationale: Scaffolding-only (RIZ-25): produces an unsigned iOS Simulator .app for internal verification; no .ipa, no TestFlight/App Store publishing. Device signing and IPA export are gated on Apple Developer Program setup and family-controls entitlement approval (RIZ-20).

nodes.label: RizeMobile XcodeGen Project Spec (iOS 17+, extension targets placeholder)
nodes.rationale: DeviceActivityReport and DeviceActivityMonitor extension targets are deliberately deferred as placeholder comments until Apple approves the com.apple.developer.family-controls entitlement (RIZ-20).
nodes.norm_label: rizemobile xcodegen project spec (ios 17+, extension targets placeholder)

nodes.label: rize-mobile Engineering Guidelines (CLAUDE.md)
nodes.rationale: Tokens live in the Keychain, never UserDefaults; local store uses iOS Data Protection completeUntilFirstUserAuthentication; extension targets stay minimal with no app-level dependencies.

nodes.label: Three-Tier Screen Time Tracking Model
nodes.rationale: Workaround for Apple's Screen Time privacy sandboxing: Tier A exact usage is rendered on-device only (Apple isolates the report extension from network and shared storage), Tier B approximate usage is derived from threshold events and synced flagged approximate, manual timers/focus sessions are exact and sync fully.

nodes.label: Tier A: DeviceActivityReport Extension (Display-Only Exact Usage)
nodes.rationale: Apple blocks network calls and shared-container writes from the DeviceActivityReport extension at the OS level; any code attempting either is an automatic HIGH-severity review finding. Exact usage data is display-only inside the extension.
nodes.norm_label: tier a: deviceactivityreport extension (display-only exact usage)

nodes.label: Tier B: DeviceActivityMonitor Threshold Events (Approximate)
nodes.rationale: Threshold-derived usage events from the DeviceActivityMonitor extension reach the app via the App Group and must be flagged precision: approximate before sync; the UI must label them as approximate.

nodes.label: App Group group.com.rizeclone.shared
nodes.rationale: Shared container used for the Monitor extension → app handoff of threshold events.

nodes.label: family-controls Entitlement Approval (RIZ-20)
nodes.rationale: com.apple.developer.family-controls works in development with the capability enabled, but App Store distribution requires Apple approval via their request form (typically weeks); this gates the extension targets and device releases.

nodes.label: Rize-Clone Git Flow (one ticket → one branch → one PR)
nodes.rationale: One Linear ticket RIZ-<n> maps to one branch feat/RIZ-<n>-<slug> and one PR [RIZ-<n>] <summary> with Conventional Commits, keeping every change atomic and traceable; never open a PR with failing tests or lint.
nodes.norm_label: rize-clone git flow (one ticket → one branch → one pr)

hyperedges.label: Offline-First Sync Pipeline (client outbox to hypertable)
Orchestrator Delegation Workflow (brief, implement, review, document)
Backend Local Compose Stack (db → migrate → api startup chain)
